citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
At the time of inspection, four rabbits were found with medical issues that the licensee had identified but was not treating
adequately and did not consult with the veterinarian on care.
- In the middle section of the second to last hutch on the parking side, a singly housed grey and white rabbit was seen
with an excessively overgrown top left tooth. Approximately 1 inch of the long tooth was visible before disappearing and
curving into the mouth, bending towards the right side. This abnormal tooth configuration caused facial distortion with a
prominent twist of the rabbit’s snout to the right. There was a significant amount of cloudy white nasal discharge on the
right nostril which per a facility representative may be caused by the discomfort created by the overgrown tooth. The
facility did document the need to watch this rabbit due to previous teeth problems but no treatment had been implemented
for his current condition.
- A white rabbit with black ears and nose in cage 177 was seen with a round open wound approximately 1 inch in
diameter below the base of the left ear. The top skin layer was missing exposing the red, shiny flesh underneath. Per a
facility representative, the wound previously oozed purulent discharge. The facility was currently treating by applying
topically ichthammol salve. Per the program of veterinary care, ichthammol salve is to be used for bruises and cuts not for
large open wounds that previously exuded purulent discharge such as this.
Two rabbits were seen with eye issues that the licensee identified but were not treated although the program of veterinary
care does describe using eye drops for such conditions.
- A tan and grey rabbit in cage 291 was observed with its left eye completely closed and a moderate amount of thick
cloudy white discharge coming out of the corner of the eye. The eyelids were red/inflamed and per a facility representative
the eye was being watched.
- A white, tan, and grey rabbit was seen with white mucopurulent discharge covering the left eye. The bottom eyelid was
significantly swollen, red and inflamed. Yellow crusty material was seen coating the hair around the eye. The doe was
seen continuously squinting. The facility was not treating because the doe was pregnant but, per a facility representative,
other safe treatment measures (shaving the fur around the eye, cleaning the eye, and eye drops) that were approved by
the program of veterinary care could have been implemented.
Medical care that is not being done per the veterinarian’s instructions can result in inappropriate methods to diagnose and
treat conditions such as those listed above. The licensee must consult with the attending veterinarian to obtain a
treatment plan for the animals noted above, and then, moving forward, in cooperation with his attending veterinarian, the
licensee must develop appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries.

At the time of inspection, signs of incompatibility were seen in several hamster enclosures.
- Two male hamsters from two separate breeding enclosures (housing one male with one female) had injuries in the
scrotum/anus area. A black and white hamster had scratches and bruises in the area between the anus and the tail as
well as a circular ulcerated red wound at the base of the right testicle. A tan and white hamster had multiple small
scratches spread across the anus, the back section of both hind legs and feet as well as a small, ulcerated lesion on the
tip of the tail. The area beneath the tail was red and inflamed.
- Aggressive behavior between cage mates was observed in several enclosures housing 2 female hamsters and hamsters
with injuries were found in two such enclosures. One cream colored hamster was found with bloody wounds spread
throughout her entire back and the bottom section of her abdomen. She had larger open wounds on her left armpit and
the left side of her nose. Another grey and white hamster from a separate enclosure had a circular ulcerated wound
beneath the right eye.
The lack of combability between hamsters housed together in the aforementioned enclosures resulted in significant
animal injury and stress. Animals housed in the same primary enclosure must be maintained in compatible groups.
Corrections were initiated at the time of inspection and must be completed by 02/12/2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
Part 1 of 2.
Additional Inspectors:
Patricia Laracuente, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2)  Direct Repeat
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
At the time of inspection, four rabbits were found with medical issues that the licensee had identified but was not treating 
adequately and did not consult with the veterinarian on care. 
 
- In the middle section of the second to last hutch on the parking side, a singly housed grey and white rabbit was seen

Medical care that is not being done per the veterinarian’s instructions can result in inappropriate methods to diagnose and

3.33  Direct
   
Classification and separation. 
At the time of inspection, signs of incompatibility were seen in several hamster enclosures. 
- Two male hamsters from two separate breeding enclosures (housing one male with one female) had injuries in the 
scrotum/anus area. A black and white hamster had scratches and bruises in the area between the anus and the tail as

The lack of combability between hamsters housed together in the aforementioned enclosures resulted in significant

Corrections were initiated at the time of inspection and must be completed by 02/12/2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives. 
 
Part 1 of 2. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Patricia Laracuente, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
